title: Chapter 4: The Pull
content: It started with the smell.

Turpentine and salt — that sharp, chemical bite cut through the warm yeast-air of Halloway's Bakery before I even turned around. My hand was already on the paper bag with my bread, already pulling it close, already knowing. And there he was at the counter behind me, ordering coffee as if it were coincidence, as if the whole of Blackwood Vale weren't small enough that coincidence was the cheapest currency we had.
"Morning," Julian said, like he'd been waiting for me to arrive.
I told myself it was nothing. A small town. People overlap.

The next morning, the cliff path. I walked earlier than usual — the sky still bruised violet, the tide a distant whisper below the rocks. I'd told no one my route. I hadn't even decided it until my feet were already moving. And there he sat on the flat stone at the overlook, sketchbook open on his knee, charcoal moving across the page in quick, certain strokes.
He didn't look surprised to see me. He looked like he'd been expecting me.
"You're up early," I said, because silence would have been its own confession.
"I like the light at this hour." He glanced up. Smiled. "You make good light."
I walked past him. I didn't stop. But I felt the pull in my chest — a physical thing, like a hook behind my sternum — and it took everything I had not to turn back.

Tuesday evening. Grimm & Root. I was locking the front door, the brass key cold in my fingers, when his shadow fell across the cobblestones. He stood on the other side of the street, leaning against the harbor wall with his hands in his pockets. Not approaching. Just waiting. Present.
"You're following me," I said across the distance.
"No." He pushed off the wall, unhurried. "I'm meeting you."
The difference should have alarmed me. It did alarm me. But underneath the alarm was something worse — something warm and gravitational that wanted me to step toward him instead of away.
Turpentine and salt. I smelled it in my sleep now.
Wednesday, he came into the shop.
Not to buy — though he did, eventually, a mother-of-pearl letter opener that he turned over in his hands with the appreciation of someone who understood materials. He came to draw.

He sat in the wingback chair by the front window and sketched while I worked. I was cataloguing a new estate consignment — someone's grandmother's things, arriving in tea chests that smelled of cedar and damp. I pulled object after object from the wrappings: a brass mantel clock, its face clouded. A pair of pewter candlesticks, tarnished the color of bruises. A tortoiseshell comb with three missing teeth. I wrote each one into the ledger, condition, estimated value, provenance notes, and he drew.
I didn't see what he was drawing. I refused to look.
"You have extraordinary hands," he said eventually.
"Practitioner's hands. I use them for work."
"That's what I mean. The way you hold each thing — like you're listening to it. Like you're asking what it remembers."
I set down the comb. I looked at him then, because he'd described something I'd never put into words, not even to myself, and the accuracy of it was a blade between my ribs.
He turned the sketchbook toward me.

It was my hands. Just my hands, holding the tortoiseshell comb, fingers splayed against the broken teeth. The rendering was precise — each knuckle, the dry patch on my thumb I got from handling old paper, the way my left index finger curved slightly inward from years of writing. True. All of it true. He'd caught the competence, the care, the quiet intensity I brought to objects that no one else could be bothered to see.

But there was more. In his rendering, my hands looked delicate. Tender. The grip on the comb could have been a caress. There was a softness in the shading around my wrists that I have never possessed — a vulnerability, as if my hands were reaching for something they might not catch.
"I see the real you," he said.
Four words. The most seductive sentence in any language. The most dangerous one, too.

Because he did see me — partly. The part that was careful and thorough and fiercely attentive to detail. But he was painting over the rest of it: the stubbornness, the suspicion, the iron framework of self-protection I'd spent twenty-six years constructing. He saw me, and then he made me softer. He made me reachable.
I felt seen. I also felt skinned.
"Nobody sees the real anyone," I said, and closed his sketchbook with my fingertip.
He smiled. Like I'd proven his point.
That night, I couldn't sleep.

The attic called me. It had been calling for days, a low hum beneath my ribs that I'd been ignoring because Julian had taken up so much of the space in my head there was barely room for anything else. I climbed the narrow stairs with a flashlight, the beams groaning under my feet, and knelt among the Buena women's things.

I'd been through most of it already — Elowen's trunk, Maren's sewing box. But there was a coat I'd set aside. Heavy wool, black, the kind of garment that survived two centuries because it was too sturdy to decay. The fabric was stiff under my fingers, the buttons oxidized to a dull green. I checked the pockets out of habit — nothing — and then felt along the lining.
A ridge. Faint, but there.

I found the seam where the fabric had been unpicked and restitched, the thread a slightly different weight than the original. My penknife opened it in one careful slice. Inside the lining, flat against the back panel: a journal. Thin. Bound in brown leather that had cracked at the spine.
The first page read: *Cordelia Buena. 1798.*
I expected prose. I got numbers.
Cordelia wrote in ledger format — neat columns with ruled lines, as precise as any accountant's workbook. Assets on the left. Debits on the right. But the categories weren't what I expected.

*Assets:*
*— House at Blackwood Vale (entailed, mine while living)*
*— Household goods, estimated value £340*
*— Skill in hex-work (see marginalia)*
*— Daughter, Marguerite, age 3*

*Debits:*
*— Husband.*
That was the entire right column. One line. But below it, in smaller script, she'd itemized:

*He stole the house through legal stricture, 1794.*
*He stole the land through deed manipulation, 1795.*
*He stole the money through power of attorney, 1796.*
*He stole the family name through marriage itself, 1798.*

Each entry precise. Each one dated. A woman documenting her own robbery in the only language the world would accept from her — the language of accounting. Because a wife's testimony was worth nothing in court. A wife's word was not evidence. But figures? Figures could be entered into ledgers, and ledgers could be preserved.
I turned the pages. More columns. More accounting. And then, in the margins of what appeared to be a household inventory, I found the hexes.

They were written in a hand slightly different from the neat ledger entries — more cramped, more urgent, pressed into the margins as though Cordelia was hiding them even from herself. The language was legalistic at first glance, each hex formatted like a clause in a contract:
*Clause the Fourth: Wherein any party shall profit by fraud or misrepresentation of assets, such profit shall return to the aggrieved party sevenfold, compounded annually from the date of discovery.*

*Clause the Seventh: Wherein any party shall conceal the true ownership of property, the concealing party shall suffer pecuniary loss equivalent to the concealed value, to be extracted from his holdings by any means available, including but not limited to poor harvest, failed venture, and the sudden failure of livestock.*

Curses hidden as clauses. Financial hexes encoded in the language of contracts. Every marginal note was a bomb designed to look like a term of agreement. Anyone reading the journal casually would see only a meticulous housewife's record-keeping. But I could read the power under the words. I could feel it humming against my fingertips, the same frequency as the hearth-magic I'd unlocked from Maren, but sharper. More specific. Money-magic. Prosperity hexes, aimed like arrows.
The power opened inside me like a door.

I sat cross-legged on the attic floor with the journal in my lap and felt it arrive — the ability to touch the flow of money, to sense where it moved honestly and where it moved through deception. I could feel the bones of financial transactions like I felt the bones of old objects. I could bless or I could curse. I could make a fair deal prosper or I could make deceit cost — sevenfold, Cordelia had written, compounded, and the word *compounded* shimmered with a witch's arithmetic that didn't obey natural law.
For one bright moment, I held it: the gift of making theft unprofitable. Of ensuring that what was taken would be repaid with interest. It was righteous power. Clean. Satisfying.
Then I turned to the final page.
Cordelia's handwriting had changed. The neat columns were gone. The words crowded together, slanting downward as though written in haste, or grief, or both:

*I used them too late. The house was gone before I hexed the deed. The land was sold before I cursed the contract. He had already spent the money by the time I wrote the first clause into the margin. I knew and could not prove — and by the time I had the power to act, the acting was moot.*
*Use them sooner, Seventh.*
*Use them the MOMENT you see the theft begin.*

I closed the journal. My hands were shaking. The attic air was cold and still and smelled of old fabric and older dust, and somewhere in the walls a mouse scratched at dry wood, and I sat there in the dark feeling the new power settle into my chest like a second heartbeat, and I thought about what Cordelia was really saying.

She wasn't just warning me. She was accusing herself. And in accusing herself, she was asking: when you see it — when you finally see the shape of the thing that's taking from you — will you be faster than I was?
The honest answer was: I don't know.

I went back downstairs. I put the journal on my desk. I stood in the kitchen and drank a glass of water and looked at my life with new eyes, the way Cordelia's ledger had taught me to look — not at what was visible, but at what was missing.
Assets first. What did I have a week ago that I didn't have now?
*My mornings.* I used to walk the cliff path alone. Now I walked it wondering if he'd be there.

*My evenings.* I used to lock up Grimm & Root and go home, or go to Nadine's, or sit with Mrs. Thorne while she told me stories about the old days. Now I stood at the door watching Julian's shadow across the street until he nodded and walked away, and then I went home and thought about him.

*My attention.* This was the biggest debit. The largest theft. I had spent more time thinking about Julian Cross in the past week than I'd spent thinking about anything else. My mind circled him the way water circles a drain — slow at first, then faster, then all at once.

I hadn't visited Nadine in two days. Two days. Nadine, who had been my closest friend since we were seven. Nadine, who I saw every single day without exception, who I told everything, who was the first person I'd ever shown Maren's hearth-magic to. I hadn't called. I hadn't stopped by. I hadn't even thought about her until this moment, standing in my kitchen at two in the morning with cold water in my hand and Cordelia's warning still hot in my chest.

Mrs. Thorne — I hadn't spoken to her since the day Julian arrived. She'd been sitting on her usual bench by the harbor, and I'd walked past with my head full of turpentine and salt, and I hadn't even stopped to say good evening.

What was he stealing? Not money. Not objects. Nothing that would show up in a ledger or an inventory or a police report. He was stealing my margins — the spaces between things where my real life happened. My time. My attention. My connections to other people. Each individual moment was so small, so unremarkable, that it didn't register as loss. But added together, they were a column of debits as long as Cordelia's.
The isolation had begun. And I hadn't even noticed.

I sat down at my desk. I opened my own journal — the modern one, the one where I'd been recording my thoughts since I was fifteen, the one that had documented every anxietress's warning I'd ever found. I uncapped my pen.

And then I sat there for a long time, because I knew what I wanted to write and I didn't want to write it, because writing it would make it real in a way that thinking it wasn't, in the way that Cordelia's columns had made her husband's theft real even when no court would believe her.
I wrote:

*Each ancestress felt chosen. Each one was isolated. Each one thought her situation was different — better, smarter, more modern. Elowen thought her gift made her special. Maren thought her hearth-magic would protect her. Cordelia thought her ledgers would save the house. And now I think my analytical mind will let me see the pattern and escape it.*
*I am not different.*
*That's the worst part. I can SEE the pattern and I'm STILL in it.*

I underlined *still* — once, twice, three times — and on the third pass the pen tore through the paper. The rip was loud in the quiet room, a sharp sound like cloth splitting, and I felt the rough edge of the torn page under my fingertip, the fibers catching against my skin, and I thought: this is what it feels like. The hole opening. The page not holding. The record failing.
I could see the trap. I could name every bar of the cage. I could describe its mechanism down to the last rivet.
And I was standing inside it anyway.

Turpentine and salt. Even now, in my kitchen at two in the morning, with no one else in the house, I could smell it. I didn't know if it was real or if I'd learned it so thoroughly that my own body was producing it now — a phantom scent generated by desire and dread in equal measure.
Cordelia wrote: *Use them the MOMENT you see the theft begin.*
I saw it. I saw it clearly, with the same precision she'd brought to her columns.

But seeing wasn't the same as stopping. And the clock was already running — had been running since the first morning in the bakery, since the first breath of turpentine and salt, since the first time I'd felt that pull in my chest and walked toward him instead of away.
I closed my journal. The torn page curled at the edge like a burned leaf.
Outside, the sea moved against the cliffs, steady and indifferent, keeping time.
